sourceText: Participates in the Gothic War in Italy.
Witnesses the outbreak of the Plague of Justinian in Constantinople.
Completes his major work, The Wars of Justinian.
Likely writes the Secret History (Anecdota).
Writes On the Buildings of Justinian.
Dies, likely in Constantinople.
The Wars of Justinian
Authored a comprehensive eight-book history of the wars fought by Emperor Justinian I, a primary source for the period.
The Secret History (Anecdota)
Wrote a scandalous secret account of the imperial court, revealing the alleged corruption and cruelty of Justinian and Theodora.
Eyewitness to History
Provided a unique, firsthand perspective on major events like the Vandalic and Gothic Wars and the Plague of Justinian.
Was Procopius loyal to Emperor Justinian?

His loyalty is complex. Publicly, in works like 'The Wars' and 'The Buildings', he praised Justinian. Privately, in the 'Secret History', he condemned him viciously, suggesting a deep disillusionment.
How reliable are his historical accounts?

His public works are generally considered reliable for military and political events, though with a pro-imperial bias. The 'Secret History' is highly biased and filled with gossip, but it offers a valuable, if exaggerated, counter-narrative to the official story.
What was his relationship with General Belisarius?

He served as Belisarius's legal advisor and secretary for many years. While his public writings praise the general, the 'Secret History' also includes criticism, suggesting a complicated relationship that soured over time.
Born in Cappadocia, son of the Exarch of Africa.
Overthrows the tyrannical Emperor Phocas and is proclaimed Emperor in Constantinople.
The Sassanid Persian army under Khosrow II captures Jerusalem and seizes the True Cross.
Personally leads a series of brilliant and daring military campaigns against the Sassanid Empire.
After defeating the Persians, recovers the True Cross and returns it to Jerusalem in a triumphal procession.
Makes Greek the official language of the Empire, replacing Latin, and adopts the title 'Basileus'.
The Byzantine army suffers a catastrophic defeat against the forces of the Rashidun Caliphate at the Battle of Yarmouk.
The provinces of Syria, Palestine, and Egypt are conquered by the advancing Arab armies.
Dies in Constantinople, having saved the empire from one foe only to see it dismembered by another.
Defeat of the Sassanid Empire
Personally led a series of brilliant campaigns that ended a 26-year war and crushed the Byzantine Empire's centuries-old rival.
Administrative & Military Reforms
Initiated the Theme system, dividing provinces into military districts, which became the foundation of Byzantine defense for centuries.
Hellenization of the Empire
Changed the official imperial language from Latin to Greek, reflecting the empire's cultural reality and marking a shift from Roman to Byzantine identity.
Why is Heraclius considered a great military commander?
He abandoned conventional strategies, personally led his troops on daring campaigns deep into enemy territory, and decisively defeated the Sassanid Persians against overwhelming odds.
What was the significance of changing the official language to Greek?
It was a major step in the transition from the 'Eastern Roman Empire' to the 'Byzantine Empire,' acknowledging that its core was Hellenic in culture and population, not Latin.
If he was so successful, why did he lose so much territory to the Arabs?
Decades of war with Persia had completely exhausted the Byzantine Empire's military and financial resources, leaving it vulnerable to the new, highly motivated Arab armies.
Born Chen Yi near Luoyang, Henan.
Fully ordained as a Buddhist monk.
Begins his journey to India, defying the Emperor's ban on travel.
Travels throughout India, studying at Nalanda University and collecting scriptures.
Returns to the Tang capital, Chang'an, with 657 Buddhist texts.
Completes his famous travelogue, 'Great Tang Records on the Western Regions'.
Leads a large state-sponsored translation project until his death.
Dies in Tongchuan, Shaanxi.
Journey to India
Undertook a 17-year, 10,000-mile overland journey to India to acquire authentic Buddhist scriptures.
Translation of Scriptures
Translated over 1,330 fascicles of Buddhist scriptures into Chinese, significantly clarifying and expanding Buddhist doctrine in East Asia.
Great Tang Records on the Western Regions
Authored a detailed travelogue that remains a primary source for the history and geography of 7th-century Central Asia and India.
Founding the Yogacara School in China
Established the Faxiang (Yogacara or 'Mind-Only') school of Buddhism in China, a major philosophical tradition.
Is Xuanzang the 'Tripitaka' from 'Journey to the West'?

Yes, the historical Xuanzang was the inspiration for the fictional character Tang Sanzang (Tripitaka) in the famous Chinese novel 'Journey to the West'. However, the novel is a fantastical allegory, and his real journey did not involve a monkey king or pig demon.
Why was his journey illegal?
In the early Tang Dynasty, Emperor Taizong had forbidden his subjects from traveling abroad due to political instability and threats on the borders. Xuanzang had to sneak out of the empire.
What was his most significant contribution?

While his journey was legendary, his most lasting contribution was his massive and meticulous translation project. His translations were of such high quality that they became the standard for centuries and profoundly shaped the development of Buddhism in China, Korea, and Japan.
Born in Chornukhy, Cossack Hetmanate, into a Cossack family.
Intermittently studied at the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy, excelling in languages, music, and philosophy.
Served as a singer in the Imperial Chapel in St. Petersburg.
Traveled through Europe as part of a mission, likely visiting Hungary, Vienna, and other centers of learning.
Taught poetics and ethics at Pereiaslav and Kharkiv Collegiums, but was dismissed due to his unorthodox methods and ideas.
Began his life as an itinerant philosopher, wandering through Ukraine, teaching, and writing.
Died in the village of Ivanivka (now Skovorodynivka), after reportedly digging his own grave the day before his death.
Father of Ukrainian Philosophy
He laid the foundations for Ukrainian native philosophy with his works on self-knowledge, happiness, and the 'three worlds'.
Concept of 'Cognate Labor' (Srodna Pratsia)
Developed the influential idea that true happiness is found by engaging in work that aligns with one's natural disposition.
Literary and Musical Works
Authored numerous philosophical dialogues, fables ('Fables of Kharkiv'), poems ('The Garden of Divine Songs'), and composed liturgical music.
Why is he called the 'Ukrainian Socrates'?

Like Socrates, he taught through dialogue, emphasized self-knowledge ('Know thyself'), and lived a simple, ascetic life, questioning societal norms without writing down his philosophy in a systematic treatise.
Did he ever publish his works?
No, none of his works were published during his lifetime. They circulated in manuscript form among his friends and followers, a common practice at the time.
What does his epitaph, 'The world tried to catch me, but failed,' mean?
It symbolizes his lifelong rejection of worldly temptations like wealth, status, and institutional confinement in favor of spiritual and intellectual freedom.
Born in Washington, County Durham, England.
Graduates from Oxford with a first-class honours degree in modern history.
Begins serious study of Arabic and makes her first major journey into the Syrian desert.
Recruited by British Intelligence at the outbreak of World War I.
Appointed Oriental Secretary to the British High Commissioner in Baghdad.
Plays a key role in the Cairo Conference, advocating for the creation of Iraq under King Faisal I.
Establishes the Baghdad Archaeological Museum (later the Iraq Museum).
Dies in Baghdad from an overdose of sleeping pills, believed to be suicide.
Architect of Modern Iraq
She was instrumental in drawing the borders of the new state and installing Faisal I as its king.
Founder of the Iraq Museum
She established the national museum in Baghdad to preserve Mesopotamia's rich archaeological heritage.
Pioneering Explorer and Archaeologist
Her extensive travels and detailed surveys of the Arabian desert provided invaluable intelligence and cultural understanding.
Was Gertrude Bell a spy?
While she worked for British Intelligence and gathered information, her role was more of a political officer and diplomat, using her deep regional knowledge to shape policy.
Why is she called the 'Queen of the Desert'?
This nickname reflects her unparalleled ability to travel through the vast Arabian deserts and her influential relationships with powerful tribal leaders.
What is her most lasting legacy?
Her most lasting, and complex, legacy is the modern state of Iraq, whose borders and political structure she helped create. The Iraq Museum is her most celebrated cultural legacy.
Born as Nader Qoli in the Khorasan province of Persia.
Enters the service of the Safavid claimant Tahmasp II and becomes his leading general.
Deposes the last Safavid ruler and is crowned Shah of Persia, founding the Afsharid dynasty.
Invades the Mughal Empire, defeats their army at the Battle of Karnal, and sacks the capital, Delhi.
Conquers Bukhara and Khiva, expanding the Persian empire to its greatest extent in the modern era.
Assassinated by a group of his own officers, leading to the rapid collapse of his empire.
Reunification of Persia
